fragments.content: Sleep disorders affect ~50 million Americans. Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA): repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep causing apneas/hypopneas, daytime sleepiness, cardiovascular risk. Risk factors: obesity, male sex, age, craniofacial abnormalities. Apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) severity: mild 5-15, moderate 15-30, severe >30 events/hour. Central sleep apnea: respiratory drive failure (heart failure, opioid use, stroke). Narcolepsy: chronic disorder of sleep-wake regulation with excessive daytime sleepiness plus cataplexy (type 1, orexin/hypocretin deficiency) or without cataplexy (type 2). Other features: sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations, disrupted nighttime sleep. Restless legs syndrome (RLS/Willis-Ekbom disease): urge to move legs with uncomfortable sensations, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement; associated with iron deficiency, pregnancy, renal failure, neuropathy. Insomnia: difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or non-restorative sleep ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months with daytime impairment. Circadian rhythm disorders: delayed sleep phase, shift work disorder, jet lag.

Sleep disorder treatment is condition-specific. OSA: CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) is first-line for moderate-severe OSA—eliminates apneas, improves sleepiness, may reduce cardiovascular risk. Weight loss critical (even 10% reduction improves AHI). Alternatives: mandibular advancement devices (mild-moderate OSA), positional therapy (positional OSA), hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire—moderate-severe OSA, CPAP-intolerant, BMI <35), upper airway surgery (uvulopalatopharyngoplasty, maxillomandibular advancement—selected cases). Central sleep apnea: treat underlying cause (optimize heart failure), adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV—contraindicated in HFrEF with LVEF ≤45%), oxygen, acetazolamide. Narcolepsy: stimulants (modafinil/armodafinil first-line, methylphenidate, amphetamines) for sleepiness; sodium oxybate (GHB) for cataplexy and sleepiness (take at night, restricted distribution); pitolisant (histamine H3 inverse agonist); antidepressants (venlafaxine, fluoxetine) for cataplexy. RLS: check ferritin (replete if <75 ng/mL); alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin—first-line); dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole—risk of augmentation with long-term use); opioids for refractory cases. Insomnia: CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia—first-line, superior long-term to medications); sleep hygiene; medications short-term only (zolpidem, eszopiclone, ramelteon, doxepin, orexin antagonists like suvorexant).

Sleep disorder diagnosis requires clinical evaluation and often sleep studies. OSA: in-lab polysomnography (PSG) is gold standard—measures EEG, EOG, EMG, airflow, respiratory effort, oxygen saturation, ECG, leg movements. AHI = (apneas + hypopneas)/hour sleep. Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) acceptable for uncomplicated suspected OSA. Narcolepsy: overnight PSG followed by Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT)—mean sleep latency <8 minutes plus ≥2 sleep-onset REM periods (SOREMPs) confirms diagnosis. CSF hypocretin-1 low in narcolepsy type 1. RLS: clinical diagnosis using four essential criteria (urge to move legs, worse at rest, relieved by movement, worse evening/night); supported by periodic limb movements on PSG, low ferritin. Insomnia: clinical diagnosis based on history; PSG not routinely indicated unless suspect sleep apnea, periodic limb movement disorder, or parasomnias. Sleep diaries and actigraphy (wearable movement monitor) for 1-2 weeks assess sleep patterns and circadian rhythms. Epworth Sleepiness Scale quantifies daytime sleepiness (score >10 abnormal). Differential diagnosis: insufficient sleep syndrome, depression, medication effects, medical conditions (pain, nocturia, dyspnea).
